Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered because I had to work around a lot of things at home. I don't have a dedicated study space, so I'd grab time whenever I could - sometimes early morning before anyone else was up, sometimes late at night. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd try to actually work through problems myself rather than just reading. I probably studied around 19 hours a week, but it was broken up into chunks whenever life allowed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening at home. Some weeks I could stick to a rough schedule, but other times family responsibilities or just not having quiet space would throw everything off. Deadlines were the main thing that shaped when I studied - I'd push harder when something was coming up. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed - like checking if a new assignment was posted or if there were updates about exams. Sometimes I'd get a notification reminder and that would prompt me to check in. I also logged in to watch the video lectures since those were really helpful for understanding concepts I couldn't get from just reading.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became sort of automatic for me - like checking my phone for messages. It helped that the course had regular content drops, so I knew there'd usually be something new to look at. The discussion posts from other students also pulled me back because I wanted to see what people were saying and contribute when I could. It wasn't a perfect habit, but it became part of my week.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me - I watched over four hours worth because I learn best when I can see things being demonstrated and worked through. Being able to pause, rewind, and really understand each step was huge since I couldn't always ask questions in person. The discussion forums were also useful because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different angles to think about things.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete a lot of the smaller assignments - my completion rate wasn't great. Some readings I'd skim or skip if they felt repetitive or if I was short on time. I had to prioritize what would actually help me understand the material for exams versus what felt like busywork. With limited resources and time, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to actually understanding the material or doing well on exams. If I could work through something hands-on and feel like I genuinely learned something new, that was valuable. Anything that felt like just checking a box without real learning behind it - I struggled to prioritize those when I had so many other demands on my time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I often couldn't afford. Required ones got my attention because they directly affected my grade, but optional stuff - even if interesting - usually got pushed aside when time was tight. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional resources that might have helped, but I had to be realistic about what I could actually get done with everything else going on.
Q9: Absolutely - a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture materials and PDFs when I had good internet access, then study them later without being connected. I also used my own notes heavily, rewriting things by hand because that helps me remember better. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or free resources online that explained concepts differently, and those wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd print out problem sets and work through them on paper for hours. Or I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes on the bus or during breaks. Some of my best studying happened when I was explaining concepts out loud to myself or working through practice problems I found elsewhere.
Q11: Yeah, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access a video lecture or reading once, download or take detailed notes, and then work with that material offline multiple times. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned early to grab what I needed and study independently. One view in the system might represent hours of actual engagement with that content.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. Sometimes family stuff would come up unexpectedly and I'd have to step back from coursework for a few days. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me manage those moments better - gave me some tools to not spiral when things got overwhelming. But yeah, life doesn't stop just because you're a student.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the biggest factor for me. When things got hectic at home or I was exhausted, the LMS was the first thing to suffer - I'd still study, but from my notes or downloaded materials instead of logging in. Confusion sometimes made me avoid certain topics temporarily until I could figure out a different way to approach them. Fatigue meant I'd do more passive watching than active participation.
Q14: There were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely - worried about other things or just too tired to focus. I'd have to rewatch sections because nothing stuck the first time. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing what people were saying. Being physically present on the platform didn't always mean I was mentally engaged.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments more than I should have - my completion rate shows that. Sometimes it was because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start. Other times I'd convince myself I could do it later, then later never came. The assignments I did complete, I put real effort into, but I struggled to keep up with all of them given everything else I was juggling.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd ramp up my activity significantly in the days before something was due - that's when you'd see me logging in more, watching videos, checking requirements. Without a deadline, I might go several days without touching the platform even if I was studying the material in other ways. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but deadlines gave me structure when I couldn't create it myself.
Q17: Feedback was motivating when I got it - seeing that my exam scores were strong reinforced that my study methods were working even if my assignment completion wasn't perfect. Positive feedback made me want to engage more, while critical feedback helped me adjust my approach. The problem was feedback sometimes came too late to really change what I was doing in the moment.
Q18: The course structure worked okay for me because it had clear milestones and the video content was well-organized. Having regular content releases gave me anchors to plan around. But the structure also assumed a level of consistent engagement that was hard for me to maintain - it was designed for someone with more stable circumstances than mine. I had to adapt it to fit my reality.
Q19: The video lectures being available on-demand was huge - I could watch them when I actually had time and focus. The discussion forums were accessible too. What made things harder was when activities required synchronous participation or assumed everyone had reliable internet and quiet study spaces. Also, having many smaller assignments was challenging for someone like me who had to pick battles carefully.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce finished work. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly, reading discussions, trying to learn - but when it comes to actually submitting something, there's a barrier. Could be perfectionism, could be life circumstances, could be not understanding what's expected. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: This is basically my story. I didn't have the highest LMS activity, but I scored really well on exams - almost 98%. That's because I was learning effectively through methods the system couldn't see. I studied my downloaded materials, worked problems by hand, really internalized concepts. The LMS only captured part of my engagement. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when people log in, what they click on, how long they're on the platform. That's useful for seeing general trends. But it completely misses the quality of engagement, offline studying, understanding versus just viewing, and all the life context that shapes why someone's activity looks the way it does. It's like measuring only what's easy to measure, not what actually matters.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm thinking about course concepts even when I'm not studying, when I'm genuinely curious about how things work. For me, engagement is about the mental investment - wrestling with ideas, making connections, wanting to master something. It's internal, and a lot of it never shows up in any system.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I studied from printed notes and downloaded materials. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent working through problems by hand or the mental effort I put into really understanding concepts. It wouldn't show that I come from a situation with limited resources where just being here and succeeding is an achievement. And it definitely wouldn't show the resilience it took to get a Distinction while juggling everything else in my life.
